content.full_text:
Re:
Fern PolrrrcAr- Pnecrrcns CoMMrssroN
428 J Street o Suite 620 o Sacramento. CA 95814-2329
(9t6) 322-5660 o Fax (916) 322-0886
August 11,2009
Michael Gogna, City Attomey
City of Healdsburg
401 Mendocino Avenue, Suite 100
Santa Rosa, CA 95401
Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-09-171
Dear Mr. Gogna:
This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Healdsburg Planning
Commissioner Alan Cohen regarding his duties under the conflict-of-interest provisions
of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1
Please note this letter is based on the facts presented. The Fair Political Practices
Commission ("the Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when it renders
assistance. (ln re Oglesby (1975) I FPPC Ops. 71.)
In addition, our advice is based solely on the provisions of the Act. We therefore
offer no opinion on the application, if any, of other conflict-of-interest laws such as
common law conflict of interest or Government Code Section 1090.
QUESTTON
May Commissioner Cohen participate in decisions involving the city's
preparation of a "special study area" plan when his personal residence will likely be
within 300 feet of the boundary of one of the study areas?
CONCLUSION
Coinmissioner Cohen may not participate in decisions involving the city's
preparation of Special Study Area 3 if his personal residence is within 500 feet of the
' The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All
statutory ref'erences are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair
Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections I 8l l0 through 18997 of Title 2 of the Calitbrnia
Code of Regulations. All regulatory ref'erences are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of
Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.
a:+,


File No. 4-09-171
Page No. 2
boundary of the study area. The "public generally" exception under Regulation 13707.10
does not apply because commissioner cohen's property does not meet all the
regulation's requirements. He may, however, participate in certain special study
decisions so long as they are not inextricably interrelited to the decislons involving
Special Study Area 3, in which Commissioner Cohen has a disqualifying conflict of
interest.
FACTS
You are the city attorney for the City of Healdsburg (the "City") and you write on
behalf of AIan Cohen, a member of the Healdsburg Planning Commiision lthe..planning
Commission").
Commissioner Cohens owns a residence in the city and the lot on which it is built
is less than one-quarter acre. Healdsburg is a general lawcity with a population of
approximately 13,000 people. It is located in northern Sonoma Couniy and is less than
10 square miles in size.
The Healdsburg City Council recently adopted an updated General plan. The
General Plan identifies five "special study areas" which arJprimarily entry ways or
"gateways" into the City. It is expected that the City Council will esiablish a..special
study area committee" consisting of two planning commissioners (among other;) to guide
the preparation of a special study area plan, including establishing the spicific boundaries
of each study area. Currently, the General Plan designates approiimate areas to be
considered for inclusion in each special study area, but the General Plan does not include
specific boundaries for any of the special study areas. It is anticipated that the Committee
will work with staff and a consultant team to assist in developing the scope of the study
and providing guidance for the process. The study, once deviloped, *ould appear before
the Planning Commission and City Council for discussion, direciion, urd porsiUte
adoption.
Mr. Cohen is a senior commissioner with 13 years of service on the planning
Commission, has been a large contributor to the City's recent General plan update, and is
an architect with experience in land use planning, housing, and other issues that will
come into play in the special study areas. You wish to get a determination regarding
whether Commissioner Cohen will be able to participati in the first special stidy area
undertaken by the City.
Commissioner Cohen owns residential property that is northeast of one of the
special study areas ("Special Study Area 3"). This property is within 300 feet of the
likely northern-most boundary of Special Study Area 3. It is anticipated that this special
study area will extend to the south and west (away form the Commissioner's residential
property) from this northern-most boundary.


File No. 4-09-171
Page No. 3
You state that it is reasonably foreseeable that the Special Study will have a
material impact on Commissioner Cohen's financial interest, namely his residential
property' You believe that Commissioner Cohen's participation in decisions involving
the study areas would be prohibited unless the "public generally exception" as described
under Regulations 18707 and 18707.10 apply.
You state that the vast majority of the study area is well beyond 300-feet from
Commissioner Cohen's residence. However, the northern-most segment of the Special
Study Area 3 will be within 300 feet of his residence.
You wish to know whether Commissioner Cohen may participate as either a
member of the Special Study Area Committee or when the pian "o-", before the
Planning Commission as a sitting commissioner. Specificuily, yo, wish to know whether
the "public generally exception," as described in Regulation iaTOZ.tO would apply in this
situation.
ANALYSIS
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions ensure that public officials will perform
their duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests
or the financial interest of persons who have supported them. (Section 81001(b).)
Specifically, Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in
making, or otherwise using his or her official position to influence a goiemmental
decision in which the official has a financial interest.
A public official has a "financial interest" in a govemmental decision, within the
meaning of the Act, when it is "reasonably foreseeable" that the govemmental decision
will have a material financial effect on one or more of the public official's economic
interests. (Section 87103; Regulation 18700(a).) In order ro determine whether a public
official has a disqualifying conflict of interest in a given governmental decision, the
commission has adopted an eighrstep analysis. (Regulation 19700(bXl)-(g).)
The general rule, however, is that a conflict of interest exists whenever a public
official makes a governmental decision that has a reasonably foreseeable material
financial effect on one or more of his or her financial intereits. (Section g7103.)
STEP ONE: IS COMMISSIONER COIIEN A PUBLIC OFFICIAL?
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions apply only to "public officials." (Section
87100.) A "public official" is "every member, officer, employee, or consultant of a state
or local government agency" (Section lz}48,Regulation iSZOf .l As a member of the


File No. A-09-l7l
Page No.4
Healdsburg Planning Commission, Commissioner Cohen is a public official within the
meaning of the Act.
STEP TWO: WILL COMMISSIONER COIDN BE MAKING, PARTICIPATING
IN MAKING, OR INFLT]ENCING A GOVERNMENTAL DECISION?
A public official "makes a governmental decision" when the official, acting
within the purview of his or her office or position, votes on a matter, obligates or
commits his or her agency to any course of action, or enters into any contiactual
agreement on behalf of his or her agency. (Regulation Lg7}z.l.)
A public official "participates in making" a governmental decision when he or
she, without substantive review, negotiates, advises, or makes recommendations
regarding a decision. (Regulation l}70z.z.) A pubric official is ..influencing a
govemmental decision" if he or she contacts, or app'ears before, or otherwisi attempts to
influence any member, officer, employee, or consultant of the city regarding the
decisions. (Regulation 18702.3.)
Because Commissioner Cohen will be considering and making recommendations
regarding the special study areas in the city, he will be making, participating in making,
or otherwise using his official position to influence a govemmental deiision.
STEP THREE: WHAT ARE COMMISSIONER COIIEN,S ECONOMIC
INTERESTS..TIIE POSSIBLE SOURCES OF CONFLICT OF'INTEREST FOR
HIM?
A public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental decision if it is
reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have material financial effect,
distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the official, a member of his or
her immediate family, or on any one of five enumerated economic interests. (Section
87103; Regulations tr8703-18703.5.) The applicable economic inrerests include:
o An economic interest in a business entity in which he or she has a direct or
indirect investment of $2,000 or more (Section 87103(a); Regulation 1g703.1(a));
or in which he or she is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any
2 If a public official's office is listed in Section 87200 ("Il2Dfilers" include city council
members) and he or she has a conflict of interest in a decision noticed at a public meeting, then he or she
must: ( I ) immediately prior to the discussion of the item, verbally identify each type of eLnomic interest
involved in the decision as well as details of the economic intereit, as discussed in Regulation
I 8702'5(bx l)(B), on the record of the meeting; (2) recuse himself or herserf; and (3) Ieave the room for rhe
duration of the discussion and/or vote on the item. For closed sessions, consent calendars, absences and
speaking as a member of the public regarding personal interests, special rules found in Regulation 1g702.5,
subdivisions (c) and (d) apply. (Section 87105.)


o
a
O
a
File No. A-09-171
Page No. 5
position of managemenr (Section 87103(d); Regulation lg703.l(b));
An economic interest in real property in which he or she has a direct or indirect
interest of $2,000 or more (Section 87103(b); Regulation 18703.2);
An economic interest in any source of income, including promised income that
aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision (Section
87103(c); Regulation 1 8703.3);
An economic interest in any source of gifts to him or her if the gifts aggregate to
$420 or more within 12 months prior to the decision (Section sTro:tel
Regulation 18703.4);
A public official has an economic interest in his or her personal expenses, income,
assets, or liabilities, as well as those of his or her immediate family. This is also
knows as the "personal financial effects" rule. (section g7103, Regulation
r8703.s.)
You have indicated that Commissioner Cohen owns a residence in Healdsburg,
within 500 feet of one of the study areas, in which we assume he has an interest of $Z,OOO
or more.3
STEP FOUR: Is coMl\[ssIoNER coIrEN's ECoNoMrc INTEREST
DIRECTLY INVOLVED IN TIIE GOVERNMBNTAL DECISION?
In order to determine if the reasonably foreseeable financial effects of the
govemmental decision on a given economic interest is material we must first determine if
the economic interest is directly involved in the govemmental decision. (Regulation
18704(a). For governmental decisions that affect real property interests -- RJgulation
18704.2 applies.
Real Property
Real property in which a public official has an economic interest is considered
directly involved if the property is located within 500 feet of the boundaries of the
property that is the subject of the governmental decision. (Regulation 18704.2(aXl).)
The map you have provided indicates that Commissioner Cohen's real property is
within 500 feet of one of the study areas proposed in the city,s general plan.
I You have not identified any other potential economic interests of Commissioner Cohen,s.
Therefore, our analysis is limited to his economic interest in his residence.


File No. A-09-l7l
Page No. 6
Accordingly, Commissioner Cohen's economic interest in real property is directly
involved in the governmental decision.a
STEP FIVE: WHAT ARE TIIE APPLICABLE MATERIALITY
STAl\[DARDS?
Real Property
Regulation 18705.2(a)(l) provides the materiality standard for directly involved
real property as follows:
"The financial effect on the real property is presumed to be material. This
presumption may be rebutted by proof that it is not reasonably
foreseeable that the govemmental decision will have anyfina-ncial effect
on the real property." (Emphasis added.)
Under this rule, the financial effect of the decision is material even if it has only a
one penny effect. This is known as the "one penny rule.', In order to rebut the
presumption, one would have to establish that the decision would not even affect the
property's value by on9 cent. Because your have indicated that it reasonably foreseeable
that the decisions involving the special study areas would have a reasonably material
financial effect on Commissioner Cohen's property, we find that this would affect the
value of his property by at least a penny, and the materiality standard has, therefore, been
met.
STEP 6: REASONABLY FORESEEABLE
An effect upon economic interests is considered "reasonably foreseeable', if there
is a substantial likelihood that it will occur. (Regulation 18706(a).) A financial effect
need not be certain to be considered reasonably foreseeable, but it must be more than a
mere possibility. (In re Thorner (1975) I Fppc ops. l9g.) whether the financial
consequences of a governmental decision are "reasonably foreseeable" at the time the
decision is made depends on the facts surrounding the decision.
You have indicated that it is reasonably foreseeable that there will be a financial
effect on Commissioner Cohen's real property.
Having determined that the governmental decision will have a reasonably
foreseeable material financial effect on Commissioner Cohen's economic interest in his
a Please note that real property that meets all the requirements under Regulation Ig704.2(bx3),
would be deemed indirectly involved. However, Commissioner Cohen's real property interest does not
meet all the requirements of this regulation because the decisions involving ttre sjeciat study areas would
concern an identifiable parcel or parcels.


File No. 4-09-171
Page No. 7
personal residence, he would have a conflict of interest that would preclude him from
participating in the decision unless an exception applies.
STEPS 7 and 8: PUBLIC GENERALLY AND LEGALLY REQUIRED
PARTICIPATION
Even if a material financial effect on a public official's economic interest is
reasonably foreseeable, he or she still may not be disqualified if the financial effect of the
governmental decision on the public official's economic interest is indistinguishable from
its effect on the public generally (Section 87103, Regulations 18700(b)(7) ;d 1g707(a)),
or if the official is legally required to participate (Section 87103; Regulation lg70g).
Public Generally: Basic Rule
Commissioner Cohen may participate in the governmental decision in which he
has a disqualifying conflict-of-interest if he meets the requirements of the basic public
generally exception under Regulation lB7 07 .1.
Under that Regulation, he must meet a two-part test by showing that the decision
would affect a "significant segment" of the public in "substantial the ,i-e *urrner.. as it
financially affects his economic interest. For decisions that affect a public official's real
property, "significant segment" is defined as l0-percent or more of alt property owners or
all residential property owners in Healdsburg, or 5,000 property owners oi r.iiO"rtiut
property owners in the city.
Once you have established that a significant segment will be financially affected,
you must show that the significant segment is financially affected in substantiatty ttre
same manner as Commissioner Cohen's property. If the significant segment will be
affected in substantially the same manner the public g"n".ully exception will apply.
You did not include sufficient information in your request to allow us to evaluate
Commissioner Cohen's real property under the basic iublic generally rule. Therefore, we
are unable to provide you with a more specific analysis.
Public Generally, Small Jurtsdictions; Effects on Official,s Domtcile.
Next we address the potential application of a specialized "public generally,,
exception to Commissioner Cohen's economic interestln his real piop"rtyl The gineral
rule, described above, is contained in Regulation 18707.1. Howevir, ihere a.e alro
several special rules that pertain to certain specific situations.
One of these is Regulation 18707 .10, which is limited in its applicarion to a public
official's personal residence, in small jurisdictions only. It provides, in pe.tinent part, as
follows:


File No. 4-09-171
Page No. 8
"The effect of a governmental decision on the residential real property
that is the domicile of a public official is not distinguishable fiornthe-
effect on the public generally if all of the following conditions are met:
"(1) The jurisdiction of the public officia|s agency has a population of
30,000 or less and covers a geographic area of ten square miles or less;
"(2)The public official is required to live within the jurisdiction;
"(3) The public official, if elected, has been elected in an at-large
jurisdiction;
"(4) The official's property is more than 300 feet from the boundaries of
the property that is the subject of the govemmental decision;
"(5) The official's property is located on a lot not more than one-quarter
acre in size or not larger than I25 percent of the median residential lot
size for the jurisdiction; and
"(6) There are at least 20 other properties under separate ownership
within a 500 foot radius of the boundaries of the pioperty that is the
subject of the governmental decision that are similar in value.',
Based on the information you have provided: the property is Commissioner
cohen's personal residence and his domicile; Healdsburg has a population of
approximately 13,000; Commissioner Cohen is requiredlo live wiitrin the jurisdiction; he
iselected at-large; his property is less than one-quarter acre in size; there *. ut least 20
other properties within 500 foot radius of the property that is the subject of the
governmental decision that are similar in value.
However, in viewing the map you have provided, it appears that Commissioner
Cohen's property is within 300 feet of one of the study ar"url3p".ial Study Area 3), and
is therefore within 300 feet from the boundaries of the subject property. In order to
qualify for the public generally exception under Regulation tS7OZ.tO,'an official,s
residence must meet all six requirements under Regulation 18707.10(a). Because
Commissioner Cohen's residence does not meet the requirements under subdivision
(a)(4) of the regulation, this exception does not apply. Therefore, Commissioner Cohen
may not participate in the govemmental decisions at issue involving Special Study
Area 3.


File No. A-09-171
Page No. 9
Because you have not presented any facts indicating that the legally required
participation exception is applicable here, we do not analyie this exception.
SEGMENTATION.
We have previously advised that large and complex decisions may, under certain
circumstances, be divided into separate decisions so thit an official who has a
disqualifying interest in one component of the decision may still participate as to other
components in which the official has no financial interest. (Regulation lglOg; Merkuloff
Advice Letter, No. I-90-542; HuffakerAdvice Letter, No. e-SO_f+:.;
Regulation 18709 provides the procedures for segmentation and states as follows:
"(a) An agency may segment a decision in which a public official
has a financial interest, to allow participation by the official,
provided all of the following conditions apply:
"(1) The decision in which the official has a financial interest can be
broken down into separate decisions that are not inextricably
interrelated ro the decision in which the official has a disquairying
financial interest;
"(2) The decision in which the official has a financial interest is
segmented from the other decisions;
"(3) The decision in which the official has a financial interest is
considered first and a final decision is reached by the agency without
the disqualified official's participation in any way; and-
"(4) Once the decision in which the official has a financial interest
has been made, the disqualified public official's participation does
not result in a reopening of, or otherwise financially affect, the
decision from which the official was disqualified.
"(b) For purposes of this regulation, decisions are "inextricably
interrelated" when the result of one decision will effectively
determine, affirm, nullify, or alter the result of another decision.',
In the past, the commissio, hus odrired that some decisions may be too
interrelated to be considered separately. For example, if the resolution of or. decision
will effectively determine the result of the other decision, the decisions may not be
segmented. (See generally Yang Advice Letter, No. I-06-198; Stone Advice Letter, No.
4-06-007; Barker Advice Letter, A-05; Huil Advice Letrer, No. 4-04-052.)


File No. A-09-l7l
Page No. 10
Segmentation may apply if the decisions regarding the study areas can be broken
down into separate decisions that are not inextricably interrelated to the decisions
involving Special Study Area 3, in which Commissioner Cohen has a disqualifying
conflict of interest. Under the guidelines of this regulation, the decision r"guraing special
study area 3 must be segmented and made before the rest of the study areas-are voted on.
You have not provided sufficient information in order for us to fully analyze this
issue. However, Commissioner Cohen may be able to participate in certain special study
decisions so long as they meet the requirements under Regulation 18709 and'are not
inextricably interrelated to the decisions involving Speciai Study Area 3, in which he has
a disqualifying conflict of interest.
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.
Sincerely,
Scott Hallabrin
Counsel
*
By: Emelyn Rodriguez
Counsel, Legal Division
ERjgl

May Commissioner Cohen participate in decisions involving the city's
preparation of a "special study area" plan when his personal residence will likely be
within 300 feet of the boundary of one of the study areas?

preparation of Special Study Area 3 if his personal residence is within 500 feet of the
, unless otherwise indicated.

boundary of the study area. The "public generally" exception under Regulation 13707.10
does not apply because commissioner cohen's property does not meet all the
regulation's requirements. He may, however, participate in certain special study
decisions so long as they are not inextricably interrelited to the decislons involving
Special Study Area 3, in which Commissioner Cohen has a disqualifying conflict of
interest.

within 500 feet of one of the study areas proposed in the city,s general plan.
I You have not identified any other potential economic interests of Commissioner Cohen,s.
Therefore, our analysis is limited to his economic interest in his residence.


File No. A-09-l7l
Accordingly, Commissioner Cohen's economic interest in real property is directly
involved in the governmental decision.a
STEP FIVE: WHAT ARE TIIE APPLICABLE MATERIALITY
STAl\[DARDS?
Real Property
Regulation 18705.2(a)(l) provides the materiality standard for directly involved
real property as follows:
"The financial effect on the real property is presumed to be material. This
presumption may be rebutted by proof that it is not reasonably
foreseeable that the govemmental decision will have anyfina-ncial effect
on the real property." (Emphasis added.)
Under this rule, the financial effect of the decision is material even if it has only a
one penny effect. This is known as the "one penny rule.', In order to rebut the
presumption, one would have to establish that the decision would not even affect the
property's value by on9 cent. Because your have indicated that it reasonably foreseeable
that the decisions involving the special study areas would have a reasonably material
financial effect on Commissioner Cohen's property, we find that this would affect the
value of his property by at least a penny, and the materiality standard has, therefore, been
met.
STEP 6: REASONABLY FORESEEABLE
An effect upon economic interests is considered "reasonably foreseeable', if there
is a substantial likelihood that it will occur. (Regulation 18706(a).) A financial effect
need not be certain to be considered reasonably foreseeable, but it must be more than a
mere possibility. (In re Thorner (1975) I Fppc ops. l9g.) whether the financial
consequences of a governmental decision are "reasonably foreseeable" at the time the
decision is made depends on the facts surrounding the decision.
You have indicated that it is reasonably foreseeable that there will be a financial
effect on Commissioner Cohen's real property.
Having determined that the governmental decision will have a reasonably
foreseeable material financial effect on Commissioner Cohen's economic interest in his
a Please note that real property that meets all the requirements under Regulation Ig704.2(bx3),
would be deemed indirectly involved. However, Commissioner Cohen's real property interest does not
meet all the requirements of this regulation because the decisions involving ttre sjeciat study areas would
concern an identifiable parcel or parcels.
personal residence, he would have a conflict of interest that would preclude him from
participating in the decision unless an exception applies.
STEPS 7 and 8: PUBLIC GENERALLY AND LEGALLY REQUIRED
PARTICIPATION
Even if a material financial effect on a public official's economic interest is
reasonably foreseeable, he or she still may not be disqualified if the financial effect of the
governmental decision on the public official's economic interest is indistinguishable from
its effect on the public generally (Section 87103, Regulations 18700(b)(7) ;d 1g707(a)),
or if the official is legally required to participate (Section 87103; Regulation lg70g).
Public Generally: Basic Rule
Commissioner Cohen may participate in the governmental decision in which he
has a disqualifying conflict-of-interest if he meets the requirements of the basic public
generally exception under Regulation lB7 07 .1.
Under that Regulation, he must meet a two-part test by showing that the decision
would affect a "significant segment" of the public in "substantial the ,i-e *urrner.. as it
financially affects his economic interest. For decisions that affect a public official's real
property, "significant segment" is defined as l0-percent or more of alt property owners or
all residential property owners in Healdsburg, or 5,000 property owners oi r.iiO"rtiut
property owners in the city.
Once you have established that a significant segment will be financially affected,
you must show that the significant segment is financially affected in substantiatty ttre
same manner as Commissioner Cohen's property. If the significant segment will be
affected in substantially the same manner the public g"n".ully exception will apply.
You did not include sufficient information in your request to allow us to evaluate
Commissioner Cohen's real property under the basic iublic generally rule. Therefore, we
are unable to provide you with a more specific analysis.
Public Generally, Small Jurtsdictions; Effects on Official,s Domtcile.
Next we address the potential application of a specialized "public generally,,
exception to Commissioner Cohen's economic interestln his real piop"rtyl The gineral
rule, described above, is contained in Regulation 18707.1. Howevir, ihere a.e alro
several special rules that pertain to certain specific situations.
One of these is Regulation 18707 .10, which is limited in its applicarion to a public
official's personal residence, in small jurisdictions only. It provides, in pe.tinent part, as
follows:
"The effect of a governmental decision on the residential real property
that is the domicile of a public official is not distinguishable fiornthe-
effect on the public generally if all of the following conditions are met:
"(1) The jurisdiction of the public officia|s agency has a population of
30,000 or less and covers a geographic area of ten square miles or less;
"(2)The public official is required to live within the jurisdiction;
"(3) The public official, if elected, has been elected in an at-large
jurisdiction;
"(4) The official's property is more than 300 feet from the boundaries of
the property that is the subject of the govemmental decision;
"(5) The official's property is located on a lot not more than one-quarter
acre in size or not larger than I25 percent of the median residential lot
size for the jurisdiction; and
"(6) There are at least 20 other properties under separate ownership
within a 500 foot radius of the boundaries of the pioperty that is the
subject of the governmental decision that are similar in value.',
Based on the information you have provided: the property is Commissioner
cohen's personal residence and his domicile; Healdsburg has a population of
approximately 13,000; Commissioner Cohen is requiredlo live wiitrin the jurisdiction; he
iselected at-large; his property is less than one-quarter acre in size; there *. ut least 20
other properties within 500 foot radius of the property that is the subject of the
governmental decision that are similar in value.
However, in viewing the map you have provided, it appears that Commissioner
Cohen's property is within 300 feet of one of the study ar"url3p".ial Study Area 3), and
is therefore within 300 feet from the boundaries of the subject property. In order to
qualify for the public generally exception under Regulation tS7OZ.tO,'an official,s
residence must meet all six requirements under Regulation 18707.10(a). Because
Commissioner Cohen's residence does not meet the requirements under subdivision
(a)(4) of the regulation, this exception does not apply. Therefore, Commissioner Cohen
may not participate in the govemmental decisions at issue involving Special Study
Area 3.
Because you have not presented any facts indicating that the legally required
participation exception is applicable here, we do not analyie this exception.
SEGMENTATION.
We have previously advised that large and complex decisions may, under certain
circumstances, be divided into separate decisions so thit an official who has a
disqualifying interest in one component of the decision may still participate as to other
components in which the official has no financial interest. (Regulation lglOg; Merkuloff
Advice Letter, No. I-90-542; HuffakerAdvice Letter, No. e-SO_f+:.;
Regulation 18709 provides the procedures for segmentation and states as follows:
"(a) An agency may segment a decision in which a public official
has a financial interest, to allow participation by the official,
provided all of the following conditions apply:
"(1) The decision in which the official has a financial interest can be
broken down into separate decisions that are not inextricably
interrelated ro the decision in which the official has a disquairying
financial interest;
"(2) The decision in which the official has a financial interest is
segmented from the other decisions;
"(3) The decision in which the official has a financial interest is
considered first and a final decision is reached by the agency without
the disqualified official's participation in any way; and-
"(4) Once the decision in which the official has a financial interest
has been made, the disqualified public official's participation does
not result in a reopening of, or otherwise financially affect, the
decision from which the official was disqualified.
"(b) For purposes of this regulation, decisions are "inextricably
interrelated" when the result of one decision will effectively
determine, affirm, nullify, or alter the result of another decision.',
In the past, the commissio, hus odrired that some decisions may be too
interrelated to be considered separately. For example, if the resolution of or. decision
will effectively determine the result of the other decision, the decisions may not be
segmented. (See generally Yang Advice Letter, No. I-06-198; Stone Advice Letter, No.
4-06-007; Barker Advice Letter, A-05; Huil Advice Letrer, No. 4-04-052.)


File No. A-09-l7l
Segmentation may apply if the decisions regarding the study areas can be broken
down into separate decisions that are not inextricably interrelated to the decisions
involving Special Study Area 3, in which Commissioner Cohen has a disqualifying
conflict of interest. Under the guidelines of this regulation, the decision r"guraing special
study area 3 must be segmented and made before the rest of the study areas-are voted on.
You have not provided sufficient information in order for us to fully analyze this
issue. However, Commissioner Cohen may be able to participate in certain special study
decisions so long as they meet the requirements under Regulation 18709 and'are not
inextricably interrelated to the decisions involving Speciai Study Area 3, in which he has
a disqualifying conflict of interest.

The Fair Political Practices Commission ("the Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when it renders assistance. (ln re Oglesby (1975) I FPPC Ops. 71.) In addition, our advice is based solely on the provisions of the Act. We therefore offer no opinion on the application, if any, of other conflict-of-interest laws such as common law conflict of interest or Government Code Section 1090. QUESTTON May Commissioner Cohen participate in decisions involving the city's preparation of a "special study area" plan when his personal residence will likely be within 300 feet of the boundary of one of the study areas? CONCLUSION Coinmissioner Cohen may not participate in decisions involving the city's preparation of Special Study Area 3 if his personal residence is within 500 feet of the ' The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. The "public generally" exception under Regulation 13707.10 does not apply because commissioner cohen's property does not meet all the regulation's requirements. He may, however, participate in certain special study decisions so long as they are not inextricably interrelited to the decislons involving Special Study Area 3, in which Commissioner Cohen has a disqualifying conflict of interest. FACTS You are the city attorney for the City of Healdsburg (the "City") and you write on behalf of AIan Cohen, a member of the Healdsburg Planning Commiision lthe..planning Commission").
